variables.content: For the two-dimensional (2D) spin-unpolarized homogeneous electron gas (HEG), defined as interacting electrons confined to two spatial dimensions with Wigner–Seitz radius $r_{s}$ defined via the area per electron, fully self-consistent $GW$ provides total energies that lie close to essentially exact diffusion Monte Carlo (QMC) benchmarks and are a substantial improvement over both the random-phase approximation (RPA) total energies and the non-self-consistent $G_{0}W_{0}$ total energies; in the low-density limit of the 2D system (large $r_{s}$), partial self-consistency ($GW_{0}$, updating $G$ with fixed $W_{0}$) matches the QMC data slightly better than full self-consistent $GW$, which indicates that diagrammatic contributions beyond those included in $GW$ (for example, vertex corrections and higher-order exchange–correlation diagrams) are more significant in two dimensions than in three dimensions, while conserving $GW$ nevertheless delivers a major improvement over $G_{0}W_{0}$ and RPA.

The finite-temperature GW-RPA on-shell calculations reproduce, in the $T\to0$ limit, the established zero-temperature RPA effective-mass results obtained in prior RPA studies of the two-dimensional electron gas, and the numerical extrapolation of the present finite-temperature results to the extreme low-temperature limit yields an effective-mass enhancement as large as $m^{*}/m\sim5$ for dilute two-dimensional electron systems at interaction strength $r_{s}\approx10$, where $r_{s}=me^{2}/(\hbar^{2}\sqrt{\pi n})$.

For the two-dimensional spin-unpolarized homogeneous electron gas (2D HEG), where electrons are confined to two spatial dimensions with the Coulomb interaction appropriate to the 2D geometry, fully self-consistent $GW$ produces exchange–correlation energies per particle that lie near diffusion Monte Carlo reference values and that constitute a substantial improvement over non-self-consistent $G_0W_0$ and over RPA total energies; however, because correlation effects and diagrammatic contributions omitted by the $GW$ approximation (vertex corrections and other neglected diagrams) are relatively more important in two dimensions than in three, the absolute accuracy of the $GW$ results is reduced in 2D compared with 3D, and in the low-density 2D regime examined the partially self-consistent $GW_0$ results are found to be slightly closer to the cited diffusion Monte Carlo reference values than the fully self-consistent $GW$ results.

Let $m^{*}(T,n)$ denote the quasiparticle on-shell effective mass computed within the GW-RPA on-shell approximation for a two-dimensional electron gas characterized by $r_{s}=me^{2}/(\hbar^{2}\sqrt{\pi n})$ and $T/T_{F}$ with $k_{B}T_{F}=\pi\hbar^{2}n/m$. The proposition is that the low-temperature linear-in-$T$ increase of $m^{*}$ (i.e. $m^{*}(T)-m^{*}(0)\propto T/T_{F}$ as $T\to0$) which is rigorously derived in the asymptotic $r_{s}\ll1$ regime persists in the numerical GW-RPA on-shell results for larger interaction strengths up to interaction parameters of order $r_{s}\sim O(1\text{--}10)$ used in the study, so that the initial linear-$T$ trend is a generic feature of the GW-RPA on-shell effective mass across these densities.

For the two-dimensional electron gas with Coulomb interaction $v(q)=\sqrt{2}\pi r_s/q$ and with an occupied-band fraction parameter $f=1/k_c^2$, the GW (RPA) approximation — i.e., using the noninteracting polarization bubble $\chi_0$ without vertex corrections or self-consistent vertex dressing when computing the screened interaction $\epsilon(q,i\omega)=1-v(q)\chi_0(q,i\omega)$ and the GW self-energy — provides a representation of screening and self-energy such that numerical evaluation of $\partial_{\epsilon}\Sigma$ and $\partial_k\Sigma$ within RPA yields estimates of the quasiparticle renormalization $Z_F$ and effective mass $m^*$ that are sufficiently accurate to capture the qualitative and the principal quantitative trends reported here for the parameter ranges (a) the high-density regime $r_s\approx0.5$–$2.0$ and (b) the low-density example values $r_s\sim6$ and $r_s\sim10$, given that prior studies incorporating several local-field corrections found only modest quantitative changes relative to RPA for similar 2D parameters.

Numerical evaluation of the GW–RPA integral formulas with the finite momentum cutoff $k_c$ shows that, for the two-dimensional electron gas, finite-band effects substantially modify quasiparticle renormalization: for the low-density example parameter values $r_s=6$ and $r_s=10$ the effective mass $m^*$ is enhanced by about a factor of 1.5 when comparing the infinite-band limit ($f=0$ where $f\equiv 1/k_c^2$) to a finite occupied-band fraction $f\approx0.8$; furthermore, across these low-density parameter values the change of $m^*$ as a function of $f$ induced by the finite band width is significantly larger than the changes produced by typical short-range local-field corrections represented by the Hubbard approximation (HA) or the modified Hubbard approximation (MHA), so that finite-band physics yields a stronger modification of $m^*$ than these short-range local-field corrections in low-density 2D electron gases.

For the total-energy values of the uniform electron gas reported in Rydberg units at density parameters $r_{\mathrm{s}}=0.5,0.7,1.0,1.5,2.5,3.0,4.0$, the GW-approximation column and the random-phase-approximation (RPA) column in the tabulated results are numerically close for each listed density (for example: at $r_{\mathrm{s}}=0.5$ GWA $=6.7084\,$Ryd versus RPA $=6.87\,$Ryd; at $r_{\mathrm{s}}=0.7$ GWA $=3.07208\,$Ryd versus RPA $=3.08\,$Ryd; at $r_{\mathrm{s}}=1.0$ GWA $=1.19788\,$Ryd versus RPA $=1.1996\,$Ryd; at $r_{\mathrm{s}}=1.5$ GWA $=0.348376\,$Ryd versus RPA $=0.3025\,$Ryd; at $r_{\mathrm{s}}=2.5$ GWA $=-2.20422\times10^{-2}\,$Ryd versus RPA $=-4.99\times10^{-2}\,$Ryd; at $r_{\mathrm{s}}=3.0$ GWA $=-7.0036\times10^{-2}\,$Ryd versus RPA $=-8.5555\times10^{-2}\,$Ryd; at $r_{\mathrm{s}}=4.0$ GWA $=-1.15673\times10^{-1}\,$Ryd versus RPA $=-9.873\times10^{-2}\,$Ryd), and this pattern of numerical proximity across the full set of densities is taken as rough agreement between the two methods.

In RPA calculations for the homogeneous electron gas in 2D and 3D, the interacting static spin susceptibility satisfies χ^{*}=g^{*}m^{*} with g^{*} the interacting Landé g-factor and m^{*} the quasiparticle effective mass; numerically within RPA the divergence of χ^{*} occurs at substantially smaller r_{s} than the divergence of m^{*} — specifically, χ^{*} and g^{*} diverge at r_{s}≈7.3 (2D) and r_{s}≈18.7 (3D) while m^{*} diverges later at r_{s}≈18.1 (2D) and r_{s}≈49.9 (3D) — and therefore, within the RPA framework applied to the homogeneous electron gas, the Stoner-type susceptibility divergence is driven primarily by a divergent interacting Landé factor g^{*} rather than by an effective-mass m^{*} divergence.

For the homogeneous electron gas of uniform density $\rho$ with Fermi momentum $p_{\mathrm{F}}=(3\pi^{2}\rho)^{1/3}$ and Coulomb coupling parameter $g\in(0,1)$, an analytical approximation to the effective interaction that includes exchange (random phase approximation with exchange, RPAE) is
      $$
      R_{\mathrm{RPAE}}(q,g,\rho)=\frac{4\pi g}{q^{2}}+R_{\mathrm{E}}(q,g,\rho),
      $$
      with the exchange correction
      $$
      R_{\mathrm{E}}(q,g,\rho)=-\frac{g\pi}{p_{\mathrm{F}}^{2}}\!\left[\frac{q^{2}}{12p_{\mathrm{F}}^{2}}\ln\!\left|1-\frac{4p_{\mathrm{F}}^{2}}{q^{2}}\right|
      -\frac{2p_{\mathrm{F}}}{3q}\ln\!\left|\frac{2p_{\mathrm{F}}-q}{2p_{\mathrm{F}}+q}\right|+\frac{1}{3}\right].
      $$
      Using $R_{\mathrm{RPAE}}$ to compute the correlation energy per electron $\varepsilon_{\mathrm{c}}$ of the homogeneous electron gas at a given Wigner–Seitz radius $r_{\mathrm{s}}$ yields results in close quantitative agreement with diffusion Monte Carlo reference values across a wide density range: for $r_{\mathrm{s}}=1$ the correlation energies (in eV) are Monte Carlo $-1.62$, RPA $-2.14$, RPAE $-1.62$; for $r_{\mathrm{s}}=3$ they are $-1.01$, $-1.44$, $-1.02$; for $r_{\mathrm{s}}=5$ they are $-0.77$, $-1.16$, $-0.80$; for $r_{\mathrm{s}}=10$ they are $-0.51$, $-0.84$, $-0.56$; for $r_{\mathrm{s}}=20$ they are $-0.31$, $-0.58$, $-0.38$; and for $r_{\mathrm{s}}=50$ they are $-0.16$, $-0.35$, $-0.22$. The RPAE approximation substantially reduces the large errors of the plain RPA, giving deviations from Monte Carlo of order 10% at $r_{\mathrm{s}}=10$, becoming nearly exact at $r_{\mathrm{s}}=1$, and tending to the exact high-density ($r_{\mathrm{s}}\to 0$) limit.

For the two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas (2D HEG), defined as interacting electrons constrained to two spatial dimensions with Wigner–Seitz radius $r_{s}$ defined via the area per electron, diagrammatic contributions beyond those included in the $GW$ approximation (for example, vertex corrections and higher-order exchange–correlation diagrams) are more important in 2D than in 3D, and, based on the computed energy differences reported for $GW$ and partially self-consistent $GW_{0}$ in the low-density 2D regime (large $r_{s}$), the $GW_{0}$ scheme may provide slightly better agreement with diffusion Monte Carlo (QMC) benchmarks than fully self-consistent $GW$ in that specific low-density limit; this statement combines a diagrammatic-importance observation with a comparative numerical observation limited to the studied density range.
